content.text: What Is Subscription Democratization?

Subscription democratization is a Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) design principle: application teams should own their own Azure subscriptions rather than sharing a central subscription managed by a platform or IT team. Under democratization, the platform team's role shifts from controlling access to resources to governing the environment through management group policy inheritance and subscription vending — a fundamentally different model of platform ownership.

The word 'democratization' is deliberate: it means distributing subscription ownership broadly across the organisation, giving each team the autonomy to manage their cloud resources without needing the platform team's involvement for day-to-day operations. The platform team retains control of governance — which management group a subscription lives in, what policies apply, what network topology is available — but not of workload resources within those subscriptions.

attrs.alt: Centralized (anti-pattern) vs Democratized (recommended) subscription ownership models

content.text: The Core Separation of Concerns
Subscription democratization depends on a clear separation between two responsibilities that are often conflated in traditional IT models:
Responsibility
Who owns it
Mechanism
Teams involved
Governance (what is allowed)
Platform team
Azure Policy at management group level; subscription vending pipeline
Cloud Platform, Security, Finance
Workload operations (what is deployed)
Application team
Owner RBAC within their subscription(s)
Development teams, DevOps engineers
Cost accountability
Application team (with platform visibility)
Per-subscription budget alerts; billing API per subscription
Application team + Finance
Network topology
Platform team
Hub VNet owned by platform; spoke VNet created by vending pipeline and handed to the team
Network / Platform team
Identity (Entra ID, PIM)
Platform team / Identity team
Entra ID tenant-level settings; PIM policy at MG scope
Identity / Security team

The platform team governs through policy and topology, not through direct access to workload resources. This is the fundamental shift that democratization requires — and the reason it only works when policy inheritance from management groups is correctly designed.
RBAC Under Democratization

In a democratized model, the application team is assigned Owner at their subscription scope, typically to an Entra ID group (not an individual). This gives them full control within their subscription — they can create resources, assign RBAC to other identities within the subscription, and manage resource locks. What they cannot do is modify or remove policy assignments that are inherited from parent management groups.

# Platform team grants Owner to the application team's Entra ID group at subscription scope
# This is done by the subscription vending pipeline, not manually

APP_TEAM_GROUP_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001'
SUB_ID='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $APP_TEAM_GROUP_OID \
  --role Owner \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID

# The platform team does NOT retain Owner on the workload subscription
# It has no standing access to workload resources
# If break-glass access is needed, PIM eligible assignment at the MG level can be used

# Verify the platform team has no direct Owner on this subscription
PLATFORM_GROUP_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002'
az role assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --assignee $PLATFORM_GROUP_OID \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

The application team can then create their own RBAC assignments within the subscription — granting Contributor to CI/CD service principals, Reader to other teams, or custom roles to specific identities. The platform team does not review or approve these assignments because the subscription boundary contains the blast radius.
Policy as the Governance Mechanism

Without direct access to workload subscriptions, the platform team governs through Azure Policy. Policy assignments at the management group level apply to every subscription in that group and cannot be removed or overridden by subscription Owners. This is the technical mechanism that makes democratization safe: teams have full autonomy within a bounded space defined by policy.

# Example: platform team assigns a deny policy at Corp MG
# that prevents public storage accounts — no subscription Owner can remove this

CORP_MG='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-public-storage' \
  --display-name 'Corp: Deny public blob access on storage accounts' \
  --policy '4fa4b6c0-31ca-4c0d-b10d-24b96f62a751' \
  --scope $CORP_MG \
  --enforcement-mode Default

# Application teams in Corp subscriptions cannot deploy a storage account
# with public blob access — the policy denies it at ARM layer

# The team CAN still create storage accounts — just not publicly accessible ones
# This is the guardrail model: restrict specific behaviours, not broad categories

# Audit mode: list non-compliant resources across all Corp subscriptions
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{Sub:subscriptionId,Resource:resourceId,Policy:policyDefinitionName}' \
  -o table
Cost Accountability in a Democratized Model

Because each team owns a subscription (or a small number of subscriptions), cost attribution is immediate and precise. Azure Cost Management aggregates costs at the subscription boundary — no complex tag-based filtering or resource group attribution is needed. The platform team's finance role becomes providing visibility dashboards, not manual cost allocation.

# Query costs per subscription for the current month using Azure Cost Management

BILLING_ACCOUNT='123456'

az cost management query \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/$BILLING_ACCOUNT" \
  --type Usage \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"SubscriptionName"}]' \
  --query 'properties.rows' \
  -o json | jq '.[] | {subscription: .[1], cost_usd: .[0]}'

# Each application team receives a budget alert when they hit 80% of their cap
# Budget was set by the vending pipeline at subscription creation
# Teams are responsible for their own cost within the budget
Addressing Common Objections to Democratization
Teams moving from a centralized model often raise practical concerns about democratization. Understanding the answers to these objections is as important as understanding the technical model.
Objection
Response
We will lose control — teams can do anything in their subscription
Control is exercised through policy, not through direct access. Teams can only do what policy permits — deny effects block prohibited actions at the ARM layer, regardless of their RBAC role.
We cannot afford a subscription per team — the subscription limit is 1,000

The EA/MCA default is 500 subscriptions per billing account, extendable to higher limits on request. Most organisations with hundreds of teams have far fewer subscriptions than they expect — one production sub per workload is a common pattern.
We will lose visibility into what teams are deploying

Activity logs per subscription, Azure Defender alerts, and Resource Graph provide complete cross-subscription visibility. The platform team retains read access via Reader at the management group level.
Teams will overspend without central budget control
Each vended subscription has a budget alert. Teams are accountable for costs within their subscription. Escalation paths and hard spend limits can be configured on the EA enrollment account.
Shared services (DNS, networking) cannot be per-subscription

Shared services live in Platform subscriptions (Connectivity, Identity, Management) owned by the platform team. Workload subscriptions consume these services via VNet peering and private DNS zone links — they do not replicate shared infrastructure.
Platform Team Access Patterns Under Democratization
The platform team has no standing Owner access to workload subscriptions in a democratized model. It retains two categories of access:
Reader at management group level — inherited by all subscriptions; allows the platform team to view resources, audit logs, and compliance state without the ability to modify workload resources
PIM eligible Owner at the root management group — activated only during break-glass scenarios (incident investigation, security response); all activations are logged and time-limited

# Grant platform team Reader at the Corp MG
# This propagates to all Corp subscriptions via inheritance
PLATFORM_OPS_GROUP='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002'
CORP_MG='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $PLATFORM_OPS_GROUP \
  --role Reader \
  --scope $CORP_MG

# This does NOT allow the platform team to create, modify,
# or delete resources in any workload subscription under Corp.
# It only allows read access for audit and visibility purposes.

# Verify the platform team has Reader but not Contributor on a workload sub
WORKLOAD_SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'
az role assignment list \
  --subscription $WORKLOAD_SUB \
  --assignee $PLATFORM_OPS_GROUP \
  --include-inherited \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

# Expected output: one Reader assignment inherited from /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp
# No Contributor, no Owner
Sandbox Subscriptions as an Entry Point

Sandbox subscriptions are the lowest-risk entry point for introducing subscription democratization in organisations that currently use a shared subscription model. Sandbox subscriptions are placed in the Sandbox management group, which has the most relaxed policy set — developers can experiment freely within a spending cap. Granting teams sandbox subscriptions builds organisational confidence in the model before rolling out per-workload Corp subscriptions.

# Sandbox subscription vending — minimal configuration
# Sandbox subscriptions: DevTest workload type, hard spending cap, no network peering

DEVELOPER_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000003'
SANDBOX_MG='mg-sandbox'

# Create the subscription (uses DevTest offer for reduced rates)
SUB_ID=$(az account create \
  --display-name "sandbox-$(date +%Y%m)-jsmith" \
  --enrollment-account-object-id $ENROLLMENT_ACCOUNT_ID \
  --billing-account-name $BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID \
  --offer-type MS-AZR-0148P \
  --query subscriptionId -o tsv)

# Place in Sandbox MG
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name $SANDBOX_MG \
  --subscription $SUB_ID

# Grant Owner to the individual developer
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $DEVELOPER_OID \
  --role Owner \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID

# Hard spending cap: $150/month; no email alerts — just hard limit
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name sandbox-cap \
  --amount 150 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date -d '+12 months' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":100,"contactEmails":["jsmith@example.com"]}]'

echo "Sandbox subscription $SUB_ID ready for developer $(DEVELOPER_OID)"
Migrating From a Centralized Model

Moving from a shared subscription to a democratized model is not a one-day change. The practical path is to adopt democratization for all new workloads immediately (every new workload gets its own subscription) while migrating existing workloads incrementally.
Establish the subscription vending pipeline so new subscriptions can be provisioned in minutes — this removes the operational cost that drove centralisation in the first place
Start with sandbox subscriptions for all developers immediately — low risk, high confidence-building impact
For existing workloads in the shared subscription, identify clean resource group boundaries that map to team ownership
Create new per-team subscriptions via the vending pipeline and migrate resource groups using az resource move or Azure Resource Mover
Update RBAC and cost reporting to point to the new subscriptions
Cancel or archive the shared subscription once all workloads have migrated

The single biggest enabler of subscription democratization is the subscription vending pipeline. Without it, provisioning a subscription requires platform team involvement and takes days — making centralisation appear cheaper. With a vending pipeline, a new subscription is ready in minutes with zero platform team interaction after the initial approval.
Terraform Module: Codifying Democratized Subscription Ownership

The following Terraform module provisions a democratized subscription structure — each entry in the `teams` variable creates a subscription, places it in the correct management group, grants Owner to the team's Entra ID group, and sets a budget. The vending pipeline calls `terraform apply` on this module when a new team subscription request is approved.

When a team PR adds their entry to `teams.auto.tfvars.json`, CI runs `terraform plan` to show the platform team what will be created. After approval and merge, `terraform apply` provisions everything atomically — subscription, MG placement, RBAC, and budget — with state tracked in Terraform. Future updates (budget change, MG move) are also PRs.
Azure Resource Graph Queries for Cross-Subscription Visibility

With subscriptions distributed across many teams, the platform team needs cross-subscription visibility for audit, compliance, and capacity planning. Azure Resource Graph queries run across all subscriptions accessible to the caller — combined with Reader at the management group level, this gives the platform team complete visibility without direct resource access.

// 1. Count resources by subscription and resource type — capacity overview
Resources
| summarize count() by subscriptionId, type
| join kind=leftouter (
    ResourceContainers
    | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
    | project subscriptionId, subscriptionName = name
) on subscriptionId
| project subscriptionName, type, count_
| order by subscriptionName asc, count_ desc

// 2. Find all subscriptions with no budget configured
ResourceContainers
| where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
| project subscriptionId, subscriptionName = name
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type == 'microsoft.consumption/budgets'
    | project subscriptionId, budgetName = name
) on subscriptionId
| where isnull(budgetName)
| project subscriptionName, subscriptionId

// 3. List subscriptions and their management group placement
ResourceContainers
| where type == 'microsoft.management/managementgroups/subscriptions'
| extend managementGroup = extract(@'managementGroups/([^/]+)/', 1, id)
| project subscriptionId, managementGroup
| join kind=leftouter (
    ResourceContainers
    | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
    | project subscriptionId, subscriptionName = name
) on subscriptionId
| project subscriptionName, managementGroup
| order by managementGroup asc

// 4. Identify subscriptions with no Owner RBAC at subscription scope
// (requires az graph query with --management-groups flag)
AuthorizationResources
| where type == 'microsoft.authorization/roleassignments'
| where properties.roleDefinitionId endswith '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635' // Owner built-in
| where properties.scope matches regex @'^/subscriptions/[^/]+$'
| project subscriptionId = tostring(split(properties.scope, '/')[2]), principalId = properties.principalId
| summarize ownerCount = count() by subscriptionId
| where ownerCount == 0
KQL: Audit Log Queries for Democratized Subscriptions

Each subscription has its own Activity Log. The platform team routes Activity Logs from all subscriptions to a central Log Analytics workspace using Azure Monitor diagnostic settings applied via policy. This enables cross-subscription audit queries from a single workspace.

// Central Log Analytics workspace — aggregated activity logs from all subscriptions

// 1. List all role assignment changes in the last 7 days across all subscriptions
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where OperationNameValue in (
    'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write',
    'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/delete'
  )
| project TimeGenerated, SubscriptionId, Caller, ActivityStatusValue,
          ResourceGroup, Resource, Properties
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// 2. Detect Owner grants — alert on any Owner assignment at subscription scope
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(1d)
| where OperationNameValue == 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write'
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Succeeded'
| extend props = parse_json(Properties)
| extend roleId = tostring(props.requestbody.roleDefinitionId)
| where roleId endswith '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635' // Owner
| project TimeGenerated, SubscriptionId, Caller, principalId = tostring(props.requestbody.principalId)

// 3. Policy violation attempts — denied deployments by team subscription
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Failed'
| where Properties has 'RequestDisallowedByPolicy'
| extend props = parse_json(Properties)
| project TimeGenerated, SubscriptionId, Caller, ResourceId,
          PolicyName = tostring(props.statusMessage)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Governance Readiness Checklist Before Launching Democratization

Introducing subscription democratization in an organisation that currently uses a shared subscription model requires the following governance elements to be in place before the first team subscription is provisioned. Skipping items here creates ungoverned subscriptions that are harder to retrofit.
Prerequisite
Why it matters
How to verify
Management group hierarchy designed and deployed
Subscriptions must land in a management group on day one — retroactive placement misses the governance window
az account management-group list shows all required MGs
Policy set deployed at each MG level
Policies must be in place before any resources are deployed in new subscriptions
az policy assignment list --scope <each MG> shows expected assignments
Subscription vending pipeline operational
Without vending, platform team becomes the bottleneck again
Run a test subscription through the pipeline end-to-end in a non-production enrollment account
Diagnostic settings policy (deployIfNotExists) assigned at root MG
Activity logs must flow to central workspace from day one of each subscription
Check policy assignment for 'Deploy Diagnostic Settings for Activity Log' at root
Budget creation is a non-optional vending step
Sandbox subscriptions especially need hard budget caps before developer access is granted
Verify vending pipeline fails if budget creation fails
Break-glass access via PIM configured at root MG
Platform team needs emergency access without standing Owner on workload subscriptions
Check PIM eligible assignments at root MG for platform on-call group
Cost dashboard available to team subscription owners
Teams need visibility into their own spend to exercise cost accountability
Confirm Azure Cost Management scope is set per subscription; team Owners can see it
Summary

Subscription democratization gives each application team ownership of their own Azure subscription, replacing central-IT-controlled shared subscriptions with a model where the platform team governs through management group policy inheritance rather than direct access control. The technical mechanisms are straightforward: Owner RBAC to an Entra ID group at subscription scope, deny policies inherited from the parent management group, and per-subscription cost budgets. The organisational challenge is cultural — shifting the platform team from a gatekeeper role to a product team that provides subscriptions, policies, and network topology as self-service infrastructure.
